title: Add new `experimental-image-proxy` hidden command
body:
This imports the code from https://github.com/cgwalters/container-image-proxy

# Why ?

First, assume one is operating on a codebase that isn't Go, but wants
to interact with container images - we can't just include the Go containers/image
library.

The primary intended use case of this is for things like
[ostree-containers](https://github.com/ostreedev/ostree-rs-ext/issues/18)
where we're using container images to encapsulate host operating system
updates, but we don't want to involve the [containers/image](github.com/containers/image/)
storage layer.

Vendoring the containers/image stack in another project is a large lift; the stripped
binary for this proxy standalone weighs in at 16M (I'm sure the lack
of LTO and the overall simplicity of the Go compiler is a large factor).
Anyways, I'd like to avoid shipping another copy.

# Immediate future

This command is marked as experimental, and hidden.  The goal is
just to use it from the ostree stack for now, ideally shipping at least
in CentOS 9 Stream relatively soon.   We can (and IMO should)
change and improve it later.

# Longer term

A lot more discussion in https://github.com/cgwalters/container-image-proxy/issues/1

comments.body: If we agree to do this I'll take a look at things like wiring up https://github.com/cgwalters/container-image-proxy/blob/main/demo.py as a test case, moving over the README.md docs etc.

> OTOH, as a matter of principle, this looks very specialized (a single-image HTTP server), and it would be somewhat a bad precedent for Skopeo, making it harder to reject a dozen more of such specialized commands

Hmm...have there been any other related requests?  

EDIT: Also let's be clear this particular one is really only intended to be used by ostree to start.  If (as discussed in the other ticket) something like https://github.com/krustlet/krustlet ends up looking at it, we can revisit things.

> Experimental or not, how do you plan to evolve the interface over time?

Well, I've been thinking about the API more, and given the desire for file descriptor passing, I think DBus may be a good fit (but happy to hear other suggestions).

Agree on the need for something like a `GET /capabilities` API or so.
But evolving the API in a bigger way I think is as easy as e.g. `--dbus-api` or `--dbus-sockfd 3` as a CLI argument for example.  (Negotiation inside a DBus API is way easier too, it already has introspection)
Is there a reason this could not be done, just talking to a podman-service rest api?

> > OTOH, as a matter of principle, this looks very specialized (a single-image HTTP server), and it would be somewhat a bad precedent for Skopeo, making it harder to reject a dozen more of such specialized commands
> 
> Hmm...have there been any other related requests?

Not of this kind, IIRC. (From time to time, there are RFEs for very specialized features that should probably be a site-specific shell script.) It’s quite fair to argue that “a matter of principle” is not a pragmatic reason not to do this.

---

> > Experimental or not, how do you plan to evolve the interface over time?
> 
> Well, I've been thinking about the API more, and given the desire for file descriptor passing, I think DBus may be a good fit (but happy to hear other suggestions).

[I’m not sure file descriptor passing makes _that_ much sense, the Go API is not very strongly tied to OS-level file descriptors, in particular the HTTP stack is fairly happy to build stacks of `io.Reader`/`io.Writer` objects implementing HTTP protocols like `transfer-encoding: chunked`, lifetime management, and the like. In particular the stream returned by `ImageSource.GetBlob` is not guaranteed to be a file descriptor.]

WRT the disk-space-saving concept of sharing a single Skopeo binary, a HTTP server would actually work better than adding a DBus dependency. But that’s not something I’m too concerned about.

> Agree on the need for something like a `GET /capabilities` API or so. But evolving the API in a bigger way I think is as easy as e.g. `--dbus-api` or `--dbus-sockfd 3` as a CLI argument for example.

Right, I don’t think it’s a big problem, just something to account for in the design. E.g. keeping the two components in exact lockstep might work perfectly fine, as long as the exact lockstep is actually consciously maintained.

> Is there a reason this could not be done, just talking to a podman-service rest api?

The REST api works on the granularity of images but doesn't allow for fetching specific manifests, configs and blobs.  But we could certainly end endpoints for that.

I have a slight preference actually do that in Podman since it's already doing REST-y things with CI in place for that etc.

> [I’m not sure file descriptor passing makes that much sense, the Go API is not very strongly tied to OS-level file descriptors, in particular the HTTP stack is fairly happy to build stacks of io.Reader/io.Writer objects implementing HTTP protocols like transfer-encoding: chunked, lifetime management, and the like. In particular the stream returned by ImageSource.GetBlob is not guaranteed to be a file descriptor.]

I more mean that we have the Go side write to a pipe, and the client read it.  And more generally, separating control and data channels is a good pattern.

> I have a slight preference actually do that in Podman since it's already doing REST-y things with CI in place for that etc.
...
> Is there a reason this could not be done, just talking to a podman-service rest api?

Well, you guys need to pick :wink: 
In
https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/10075#issuecomment-822509112
it was argued to move this to skopeo...

Though, I do notice podman already [vendors godbus](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/703db8e9a884966f5b297f9450735ee4263dd072/go.mod#L34) whereas skopeo doesn't.
Bringing @mheon since he blocked that PR.  To get his opinion.
FWIW if this ends up in either of Podman/Skopeo (and does not introduce, vaguely, excessive dependencies), I’m personally fine with it being added to Skopeo rather than Podman.

I continue to be of the opinion that, feature-wise, Skopeo is a better fit than Podman - this still strikes me as a dedicated image management command, and that's definitely Skopeo's area of specialty. However, given this is only a hidden command, if the maintainers here are strongly against including it, I won't argue too strongly about including in Podman.
FWIW, I'd vote for keeping this in Skopeo.
OK, Skopeo it is.

> OK, Skopeo it is.

OK, let’s do this.

@cgwalters If this is for exclusive use of `ostree-containers`, with no API stability commitments, and `ostree-containers` maintainers are going to to maintain/update this Skopeo subcommand as necessary: given how it doesn’t affect anything else and adds no dependencies, it could be basically merged right now as is — we’ll defer to you on all questions of API form / API stability / code quality of this.

---

Elsewhere you mentioned that you are trying an entirely different interface — do you want to move this PR to a draft status in the meantime, so that it doesn’t get merged in the current form?

Copying from https://github.com/cgwalters/container-image-proxy/issues/1#issuecomment-938694680

OK I spent about 2 hours trying to do a DBus interface
https://github.com/cgwalters/container-image-proxy/tree/dbus
and what I ended up hitting is that the Go DBus bindings are simply not prepared to act as a "raw server" in the DBus sense.

It's not trivial to implement.

Then I tried to do the "fake net.Listener" but ran into issues I had trouble debugging in the Go http flow, and it was clearly a hack.  

And then what I realized is that there's the better option for this case I used for https://github.com/coreos/bootupd/blob/main/src/ipc.rs - SOCK_SEQPACKET where we do something super simple like JSON inside.

I've updated this PR to implement that new protocol, and I also have some Rust client code in https://github.com/cgwalters/container-image-proxy/blob/seqpacket/example-rust/src/main.rs

Unless anyone has further thoughts/objections I'll work on refining this more and e.g. try to move over the README.md docs and have some basic tests as part of this PR.  I also need to go through and address the non-IPC-mechanics part of the PR review so far.

OK https://github.com/ostreedev/ostree-rs-ext/pull/118 is updated to use this.

> Just to confirm, this looks good to me.

Thanks!  I'm still working through the backlog of your comments, in particular I think the the OCI-upconverting is a must-fix.  But I think I should be done with it all by today.
I split out the Rust client code I've been working on into a separate sub-crate; I plan to publish to crates.io at some point.  See later commit in https://github.com/ostreedev/ostree-rs-ext/pull/118

The osx CI failure looks spurious, right?

Other than that I think this should be good to go if there are no other comments.  Thanks for all the detailed review so far!

> The osx CI failure looks spurious, right?

Can you run `gofmt -s -w $files` with Go 1.17? This looks like https://go.googlesource.com/proposal/+/master/design/draft-gobuild.md — but it’s weird that the `validate` test did succeed.

>  This looks like https://go.googlesource.com/proposal/+/master/design/draft-gobuild.md 

Ah, wow.  I read most of that and sure, the C preprocessor has traps, but the Rust style [cfg](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/macro.cfg.html) approach seems just better.

Anyways, updated for latest comments!

OK I've split out the Rust bindings to https://github.com/cgwalters/containers-image-proxy-rs

Would it make sense to move this into the containers/ GH organization?

I've been thinking about this whole thing more, and I suspect at least [krustlet](https://github.com/krustlet/krustlet) could probably use this too mostly as is.  However, another obvious use case occurred to me around e.g. [the containrs project](https://github.com/containers/containrs/tree/master/crates/image).  And there's an interesting question there around whether such a project would want to do a similar RPC style thing for containers/storage, or do that on its own.

And another interesting sub-thread here is: we could probably significantly slim down skopeo by having it delegate e.g. `containers-storage:` backend via a similar RPC to `/usr/bin/podman`.  Among other things, no more skopeo dependency on e.g. `btrfs-devel` etc.  (I can't think of any sane use case for having `skopeo` but not `podman` and wanting to write to `containers-storage:)

I've also been thinking about write support...it would seem natural to do, but honestly not urgent.  What we do right now in writing an `oci:tempdir` and then forking `skopeo copy` is inefficient, but it just happens on the "build side" and there's already a lot of other slow parts there.  I care a lot more about e.g. streaming reads on the client side.

Anyways, there's a lot of bigger picture questions opened by this.  We don't need to solve any of them now - simply having this ship in Fedora/C9S in the relatively near future will unblock my immediate needs.    But I'm just writing down things here to think about.

> And there's an interesting question there around whether such a project would want to do a similar RPC style thing for containers/storage, or do that on its own.

Doesn’t that end up pretty similar to CRI-O, in size and dependency extent? Sure, it doesn’t have quite the same RPC interface, and combining the functionality of CRI-O + Kubelet into a single daemon sounds appealing at least at a first glance. But I’d expect that a `PullImage` RPC could, for these uses, work at least as well as a detailed RPC over c/image + the c/storage API surface.

> And another interesting sub-thread here is: we could probably significantly slim down skopeo by having it delegate e.g. `containers-storage:` backend via a similar RPC to `/usr/bin/podman`.

[Well, we want the world of shared libraries again :) and we can’t get that, it seems.]

c/storage has been the _primary_ driver for API extensions to `types.ImageDestination` over time; that’s not to say that creating a RPC for writes to that would be impossible, but the new features / efficiencies gained by those API extensions could be lost if passed through a stable/slower/less-maintained RPC. 

> (I can't think of any sane use case for having `skopeo` but not `podman` and wanting to write to `containers-storage:)

I could similarly argue that all c/storage use should be through `podman` with its fairly extensive config/environment defaults, most of which Skopeo does not use. To an extent, Skopeo’s support for c/storage was just an automatic result of having `alltransports.ParseImageName`, and the idea that this function should have the same semantics everywhere; it actually took some extra patching quite a bit of time later later to make it work at all. So it’s not _too_ appealing to maintain a changing RPC protocol just the rare use cases of Skopeo (and unlike c/image, c/storage does change its API in a not-100%-compatible way, IIRC).

Surprisingly uses of `skopeo` + c/storage show up from time to time, but they are somewhat rare and I don’t have a good handle of whether they have something significant in common (are they for debugging? for non-Podman uses? for completely unanticipated uses of Skopeo + c/storage)?
BTW it is possible to compile-out (most of) the c/storage transport, for system builders that care to that level. ( https://github.com/containers/image/issues/1395 )

> and unlike c/image, c/storage does change its API in a not-100%-compatible way, IIRC

Oops, that’s of course irrelevant because the RPC would be over the c/image transport concept, not the c/storage API.
